product_overview: ZTBrowser is a developer trust platform that lets a browser verify an enclave-backed execution claim, enrich that claim with public provenance metadata, and support repeatable deployment of attested services on AWS Nitro and AWS CoCo.

general_info.name: Browser-visible attestation verification
general_info.overview: The extension fetches attestation from the current origin, verifies the attestation document, and shows a lock or failure reason.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Give users and operators a browser-visible trust decision for enclave-backed services.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Attestation is usually backend-oriented and difficult to verify from the browser.

actors_and_roles.secondary_user: End user consuming a trust-marked site

business_rules.description: The lock is shown only after successful cryptographic verification and nonce match.
Facts lookup may enrich metadata but must never override a cryptographic verification failure.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a user, I want the extension to show a lock only when the attestation is valid, so that I do not trust unverifiable services.

bdd.given: A page origin exposes a valid attestation endpoint
and_preconditions: The extension can fetch the endpoint
The trusted root set contains the required root certificate
bdd.when: The extension validates the returned attestation document for the generated nonce
bdd.then: The extension stores workingEnv=true and codeValidated=true and shows a locked state
and_postconditions: Verified realizations are available for optional facts lookup

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a user, I want invalid or malformed attestation to remain unlocked with a reason, so that failures are visible rather than silent.

bdd.given: A page origin returns an invalid or malformed attestation response
bdd.when: The verifier detects invalid signature, invalid chain, bad payload, or unsupported platform
bdd.then: The extension stores an unlocked state and a structured failure reason

non_functional_requirements.description: Verification must happen inside the extension runtime or another trusted local component, not in the remote page context.
Failure reasons must be inspectable through the popup and debug trace.

test_cases.setup: Load a valid attestation document and matching nonce

general_info.name: Facts and provenance lookup
general_info.overview: The extension maps verified realizations to public workload and release metadata.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Explain what measured workload identity the browser has verified.
goal_and_context.user_problem: A valid attestation document alone does not tell users which repo or release the service corresponds to.

business_rules.description: Facts lookup is keyed by verified realization identity, not by repo name or user input.
A facts miss must not invalidate a successful cryptographic verification result.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a reviewer, I want a verified realization to resolve to workload metadata when available, so that I can understand what was measured.

bdd.given: The verifier has already produced realization values
and_preconditions: The facts node contains a matching row
bdd.then: The extension stores factsMatched=true and workload metadata
and_postconditions: The popup can show repo URL and image digest

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a user, I want the extension to stay locked even when facts lookup misses, so that metadata outages do not falsify cryptographic verification.

bdd.given: The verifier has produced a successful verdict but the facts node has no matching row or is unavailable
bdd.then: The extension keeps the cryptographic success state and records factsMatched=false
and_postconditions: The popup may show no workload metadata

non_functional_requirements.description: Facts rows must be treated as transparency metadata, not as a cryptographic root of trust.
Facts schema must support canonical release provenance fields without breaking lookup compatibility.

test_cases.setup: Provide verified realization data and a matching facts row
Provide verified realization data and an unavailable or mismatching facts service
test_cases.assertion: lock remains based on verification and factsMatched=false

general_info.name: Canonical enclave release provenance
general_info.overview: The measured workload is owned by a dedicated enclave repo that publishes canonical artifacts and facts updates.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Make runtime measurement claims auditable and reproducible.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Manual workspace builds and monorepo-level identity make PCR claims ambiguous and hard to audit.

business_rules.description: Canonical facts rows must come from tagged enclave release CI, not from manual local builds.
The measured enclave source is the dedicated enclave repo, not the entire integration monorepo.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want a tagged enclave release to generate canonical artifacts and a facts PR, so that the deployed identity is traceable.

bdd.given: A release tag is pushed in the canonical enclave repo
bdd.then: The EIF, measurements, provenance manifest, and facts-row update are produced
and_postconditions: A PR can update canonical facts in ztbrowser

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a reviewer, I want rebuild verification against published provenance, so that I can compare current outputs against the release artifact set.

bdd.given: A published provenance manifest exists and a rebuild verifier is available
and_preconditions: The same repo and ref are available
bdd.when: The same repo and ref are rebuilt
bdd.then: The EIF and measurement outputs can be compared against the published manifest

non_functional_requirements.description: Canonical provenance shall be reproducible and auditable.
Release and facts automation shall minimize accidental cloud cost.

test_cases.assertion: Release artifacts and facts PR are produced

general_info.name: Operator deployment workflow
general_info.overview: Operators can deploy and verify tagged releases via AWS automation and inspect live deployments through a TUI.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Make canonical deployment repeatable and observable.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Attested deployment workflows are difficult to run consistently from shell steps alone.

actors_and_roles.system_service: AWS CLI scripts and ztdeploy TUI

business_rules.description: The operator flow must preserve deploy, verify, cleanup, and managed-list semantics.
AWS CoCo is experimental in v1 but uses the same operator surface as the Nitro baseline.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want the verify flow to deploy, check, and clean up automatically, so that I can validate a release repeatably.

bdd.given: The AWS profile and release tag are configured
bdd.when: The operator runs the verify flow
bdd.then: The workload is deployed, verified, and then stopped or terminated based on cleanup settings
and_postconditions: Stage logs and verification status are visible

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want to inspect and manage live deployments from the TUI, so that I can observe lifecycle actions.

and_preconditions: The TUI is connected to the AWS account
bdd.when: The operator opens the deployments view
bdd.then: The deployments view lists instances and lifecycle actions
and_postconditions: Stop and terminate actions remain available

non_functional_requirements.description: Deployment automation shall minimize accidental cloud cost.
Release and operator metadata shall preserve traceability across Nitro and AWS CoCo realizations.

test_cases.setup: Configure release tag and AWS profile

general_info.name: Demo and self-signed application path
general_info.overview: Demo services expose Nitro-shaped attestation for learning and exploration while remaining clearly noncanonical.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Keep a noncanonical learning path available without diluting canonical trust claims.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Demo services need distinct trust-root labeling so that they are not confused with canonical releases.

business_rules.description: Demo services shall remain visibly noncanonical.
Demo attestation shape may match the canonical path, but the trust root must remain clearly different.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a demo operator, I want a self-signed path that still exercises the browser verification flow, so that I can demo the UX.

bdd.given: Micrus is running with its demo root
and_preconditions: The extension is configured for the demo origin
bdd.when: The browser visits the demo page
bdd.then: The page can be verified or intentionally rejected according to the configured demo root

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a presenter, I want canonical and demo keychains to stay visibly distinct, so that trust-root differences are obvious.

bdd.given: The popup renders a verified state
and_preconditions: The current platform or trust root is known
bdd.then: The popup distinguishes demo and canonical trust roots or equivalent platform labeling as implemented
and_postconditions: The user can tell demo from canonical

non_functional_requirements.description: Demo paths shall remain clearly labeled as noncanonical.

test_cases.setup: Load the popup with demo and canonical states

general_info.name: Multi-backend service realizations and AWS CoCo integration
general_info.overview: One release can be realized on AWS Nitro or AWS CoCo using a common envelope and release-centered metadata.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Support the same canonical HTML service release on AWS Nitro and AWS CoCo without rewriting the product model.
goal_and_context.user_problem: A PCR-only model cannot express a workload-specific CoCo realization or a release with multiple accepted backend realizations.

actors_and_roles.system_service: Extension verifier, facts-node, ztdeploy, and release workflow

business_rules.description: The public attestation contract uses a versioned common envelope with a public platform field.
AWS CoCo evidence must be workload-specific and normalized to image digest plus measured Init-Data hash.
Nitro compatibility must remain available while release-centered realization lookup is added.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a reviewer, I want one attestation endpoint contract for Nitro and CoCo, so that browser entrypoints do not depend on backend-specific UX.

bdd.given: The service exposes a versioned common envelope
bdd.then: The verifier dispatches to the correct backend-specific path
and_postconditions: The result is normalized for browser storage

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a security engineer, I want CoCo evidence to prove a workload-specific identity rather than a generic guest, so that browser-visible trust is not vulnerable to interchangeable evidence.

bdd.given: The CoCo verifier receives raw evidence
and_preconditions: The evidence is bound to a workload-specific runtime
bdd.then: The identity becomes `image_digest + initdata_hash` and generic evidence is rejected
and_postconditions: The browser can match the realization to release metadata

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a reviewer, I want release-centered facts lookup across accepted realizations, so that one release can describe both Nitro and CoCo.

bdd.given: A release has accepted Nitro and CoCo realizations
and_preconditions: The facts database contains the release record
bdd.when: The extension queries the release-realization lookup
bdd.then: The facts node resolves the realization and still preserves Nitro compatibility

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want the canonical HTML service to lower into Nitro and AWS CoCo deployment inputs, so that I can deploy either realization from one release.

bdd.given: The canonical release repo publishes a release manifest
and_preconditions: Nitro and CoCo lowering artifacts are generated
bdd.then: The release publishes Nitro and CoCo realization outputs
and_postconditions: The release artifacts remain versioned and auditable

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want an AWS-only experimental CoCo deploy/verify path, so that I can prove the backend actually works rather than only generating code or docs.

bdd.given: The AWS CoCo lane is enabled in the operator surface
and_preconditions: A real AWS substrate is available
bdd.then: The service deploys, the landing page and attestation endpoint are checked, and cleanup happens according to mode
and_postconditions: The run is marked experimental, not production-proof

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want the runtime and operator UI to reveal the backend realization in use, so that Nitro and CoCo runs are not confused.

bdd.given: Nitro and CoCo runs may both exist
bdd.when: The operator inspects the run list
bdd.then: The platform metadata is visible and distinguishes the realizations

non_functional_requirements.description: Common-envelope and realization identity changes shall remain versioned and extensible.
AWS CoCo evidence shall remain workload-specific and must not rely on generic guest evidence.
Facts shall remain metadata even when multiple accepted realizations exist for a release.
AWS CoCo operator support shall remain explicitly experimental in the current product scope.
The new CoCo path shall not silently replace or regress Nitro verification semantics.
Release and operator metadata shall preserve traceability across Nitro and AWS CoCo realizations.

test_cases.setup: Provide an unknown platform or bad envelope version
Provide a matched realization and facts row
Provide a facts miss after successful verification

test_cases.assertion: Nitro and CoCo realization outputs are present

test_cases.setup: Fetch runtime envelope from the endpoint
Have mixed Nitro and CoCo managed runs

test_cases.assertion: Lifecycle state and platform are visible
Later allowed instance type is selected
Instance type persists into run metadata
